text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPHASIC)

CLINICAL INDICATION: 66-year-old male with new diagnosis of pancreatic head mass, EUS-FNA confirming adenocarcinoma. Pancreatic protocol CT obtained for surgical staging and vascular mapping. Evaluate resectability. Elevated serum CA 19-9. History of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis performed with IV contrast (120 mL iohexol, 350 mg I/mL) following a pancreatic protocol with three acquisition phases: non-contrast, pancreatic parenchymal phase (35-second delay), and portal venous phase (70-second delay). Thin-section 0.6 mm axial reconstructions performed with multiplanar reformats in coronal and sagittal planes. Curved planar reformations of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), superior mesenteric vein (SMV), portal vein, and celiac axis were generated. Water administered orally prior to examination for gastric/duodenal opacification.

COMPARISON: No prior cross-sectional abdominal imaging on file.

---

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS:
There is an ill-defined, poorly marginated, hypovascular mass centered in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas measuring approximately 3.8 cm in greatest axial dimension (craniocaudal extent approximately 3.2 cm). On the pancreatic parenchymal phase, the mass demonstrates markedly reduced enhancement relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma, appearing as a hypodense infiltrative lesion with irregular, spiculated margins and associated desmoplastic reaction. There is no discrete capsule. The mass does not demonstrate appreciable internal calcification. The remaining pancreatic body and tail demonstrate moderate parenchymal atrophy, attributable to upstream obstruction. The pancreatic duct in the body and tail is dilated, measuring up to 6 mm in diameter, consistent with obstructive ductal dilation from the mass. No additional pancreatic lesions are identified.

VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS (key findings for surgical planning):

Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA): The tumor demonstrates direct contact with the right lateral and posterior wall of the SMA over an arc of approximately 120 to 150 degrees of circumference. The fat plane between the tumor and the SMA is obliterated along this segment, however there is no definite irregularity or narrowing of the SMA lumen. The contact spans approximately 1.5 cm in craniocaudal extent. The SMA origin from the aorta is widely patent. The inferior pancreaticoduodenal arcade, while encased in the region of the mass, does not show occlusion.

Superior Mesenteric Vein (SMV) and Portal Vein Confluence: Short-segment contact between the right lateral wall of the SMV and the medial aspect of the tumor is present, spanning approximately 1.0 to 1.2 cm. There is focal loss of the intervening fat plane with subtle irregularity of the SMV wall at this location, raising concern for early mural involvement. The SMV lumen appears patent without definite thrombosis; however, the interface between tumor and vein is indistinct on the parenchymal phase. The confluence of the SMV and portal vein is similarly involved with a short segment of apparent abutment; no venous occlusion or collateral venous development is identified. The main portal vein above the confluence is patent and normal in caliber.

Celiac Axis and Hepatic Artery: The celiac axis origin and common hepatic artery are patent and widely separated from the tumor. No tumor contact with the celiac axis or its branches. The proper hepatic artery, left hepatic artery, and right hepatic artery all demonstrate normal caliber and course. A replaced right hepatic artery is NOT identified. No accessory hepatic arterial anatomy of surgical consequence is noted.

Aorta and Inferior Vena Cava: The abdominal aorta is mildly ectatic with mild atherosclerotic calcification, without aneurysmal dilation. The inferior vena cava is patent. No aortocaval adenopathy.

BILIARY SYSTEM:
The common bile duct is mildly dilated, measuring up to 10 mm in the distal common bile duct just proximal to its entry into the pancreatic head mass. The intrahepatic bile ducts are borderline prominent bilaterally, consistent with mild proximal biliary obstruction. No cholelithiasis or choledocholithiasis identified. The gallbladder is present, nondistended, and without intraluminal stones or wall thickening. No pneumobilia.

LYMPH NODES:
Two periportal/hepatoduodenal ligament lymph nodes are identified, the largest measuring approximately 1.2 cm in short axis. These are mildly rounded in morphology and demonstrate slightly increased density on portal venous phase imaging, raising concern for metastatic nodal involvement. There is no bulky adenopathy in the retroperitoneum, mesentery, para-aortic, or aortocaval regions. No pelvic lymphadenopathy.

LIVER:
The liver is normal in size with smooth contours. The hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous in attenuation on all three phases. No focal hepatic lesion is identified. No arterially enhancing lesion, no hypoenhancing mass, and no hepatic cyst. No biloma. No evidence of hepatic metastases.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal in size and morphology. No adrenal nodule or mass.

SPLEEN:
Spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No splenic lesion.

KIDNEYS:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and enhancement pattern. No hydronephrosis, renal calculi, or solid renal mass. Bilateral renal cortical cysts measuring up to 1.0 cm, all too small to fully characterize but with simple CT attenuation characteristics.

STOMACH AND DUODENUM:
The stomach is decompressed and unremarkable. The duodenum is extrinsically compressed by the pancreatic head mass along the medial wall of the second portion (C-loop), with no definite mucosal invasion identified on this examination. No duodenal obstruction.

SMALL BOWEL AND COLON:
No small bowel obstruction. No bowel wall thickening. No mesenteric masses. The colon and rectum appear unremarkable.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
No peritoneal implants or nodules are identified. No omental caking. No free intraperitoneal fluid or ascites.

BONY STRUCTURES AND SOFT TISSUES:
No lytic or blastic osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic disease. Mild degenerative changes of the lumbar spine. No pathologic fracture. Abdominal wall and soft tissues are unremarkable. No inguinal hernia.

PELVIS (portal venous phase only):
No pelvic mass or free fluid. Bladder is normally distended. Prostate is mildly enlarged, consistent with benign prostatic hypertrophy in this age group.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. ILL-DEFINED HYPOVASCULAR MASS IN THE PANCREATIC HEAD AND UNCINATE PROCESS, measuring approximately 3.8 cm, with imaging features strongly characteristic of ductal adenocarcinoma in the clinical setting of biopsy-confirmed malignancy.

2. BORDERLINE RESECTABLE PANCREATIC HEAD ADENOCARCINOMA by NCCN/AHPBA imaging criteria:
   - SMA: Tumor contact involving approximately 120-150 degrees of circumference without definite luminal irregularity; no arterial encasement exceeding 180 degrees.
   - SMV/Portal Vein Confluence: Short-segment tumor abutment with focal loss of fat plane and possible subtle mural contact; vessel remains patent without occlusion. Venous reconstruction may be required at surgery.

3. OBSTRUCTIVE PANCREATIC AND BILIARY DUCTAL DILATION: Main pancreatic duct dilated to 6 mm in the body and tail; mild common bile duct dilation to 10 mm, consistent with double-duct sign physiology given the location of the mass.

4. TWO SUSPICIOUS PERIPORTAL LYMPH NODES measuring up to 1.2 cm in short axis; morphology and size raise concern for regional nodal metastases, though definitive characterization is not possible on CT alone.

5. NO HEPATIC METASTASES.

6. NO PERITONEAL CARCINOMATOSIS OR ASCITES.

7. NO DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE IDENTIFIED ON THIS EXAMINATION.

Overall staging impression: Locally advanced pancreatic head adenocarcinoma, consistent with cT3N1M0 (Stage III), borderline resectable by current imaging criteria. Vascular map provided above for surgical planning purposes. Correlation with serum CA 19-9 and multidisciplinary hepatopancreatobiliary tumor board evaluation recommended prior to definitive management decision.
